Memorandum

Alan Herndon 
Date: January 27, 1978 
Fair Political Practices Commiss 
From 
: Secretary 
- DAVID B. PITMAN, Manager 
Political Reform Division 
Subject: Consoli dated Reports Fi 1 ed by Lobbyi s tEmp 1 oyers 
In April, 1977, we were given qualified approval by the Commission staff to 
allow consolidated statements to be filed by Chevron USA, Inc. and Standard 
Oil Company of California based on the determination that they were "acting 
in concert." 
We have the situation where Southern California Rock Products Association 
and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association have been filing 
consolidated statements. 
I understand that you have advised this office 
that in this instance such consolidation should not be allowed. 
At the present time, Southern California Rock Products Association and 
Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association file separate reports. 
Separate reporting began with the September, 1977, report at the suggestion 
of R. Slagle, an auditor with the Franchise Tax Board. 
In view of Government Code Sections 86108(a) and 82047, we have allowed 
lobbyist employers to file consolidated reports. 
Would you specifically 
advise with respect to reports submitted by Southern California Rock 
Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Asso-
ciation why consolidation is inappropriate and provide guidelines to this 
agency on when such consolidations should be allowed.

Attachment - Period 12/1/77 through 12/31/77 
Explanation With Respect To Items Identified With An Asterisk (*). 
1. 
It is assumed that to influence or attempt to influence 
administrative action requires a "communication" with regard to a 
"proceeding." 
Thus, only "proceeding" numbers which have been the 
subject of communications (including agreeing to hearing dates, 
filing pleadings, etc.) are listed. 
None of the proceedings are 
rate making proceedings in the usual sense of the term. 
However, 
the question of whether other proceedings before the Public 
Utilities Commission are "quasi-legislative" or "quasi-judicial" is 
often a difficult question which has only been partially clarified 
by regulations issued to date and which may be subject to dispute in 
many instances. 
To be conservative, in all questionable cases the 
proceeding has arbitrarily been treated as being quasi-legislative 
and some or all of the proceedings listed may actually be entirely 
judicial in nature. 
For this reason, much or all of the amounts shown 
for payments received and payments made may actually be erroneously 
reported because not in any way connected with attempts to influence 
"administrative action". 
2. 
In Column (a) on page 1, silari has been allocated by 
computing the number of days covered by the report, excluding 
Saturdays and Sundays (but not excluding holidays, vacation days or 
days not worked because of illness) and, assuming that each day is 
a 7-1/2 bour day, dividing the total into the hours expended in 
"connection", directly or indirectly, with what might possibly be 
considered to be lobbying activities to Qbtain a percentage which, 
in turn, was applied to the salary received for the period covered 
by the report. 
This appears to be the only reasonably practical 
method and almost certainly allocates a larger portion of salary to 
"lobbying" than is actually the case. 
3. 
All amounts shown in Column (c) on page 1 are either advances 
or airline tickets (a personal maintenance expense) charged directly 
to the employers account. 
In addition to salary and advance(s) shown 
under Columns (a) and (c), on page 1, other various fringe benefits 
were received, such as medical insurance, etc., the value of which 
is impossible and impractical to calculate or obtain for purposes 
of the lobbyist report and therefore have not been included. 
Also 
since only a portion of my time as an attorney-at-law is spent in 
activities related to the handling of proceedings before the 
California Public Utilities Commission, the amount shown as an advance 
is an allocated portion of total advances based upon ~stimate of .", 
~mount reasonably necessary to cover personal expense~ to be incurred.

.

4. 
To the extent possible, all disbursement items are posted 
and reported according to date incurred rather than date paid. 
Personal maintenance expenses, including reimbursement for use of 
personal automobiles, are reported in amounts reimbursed by employer 
since no other possible or practical method is available and any 
attempt to use any other basis would result in providing less 
accurate information.

Attachment - Period 12/1/77 through 12/31/77 
Explanation With Respect To Items Identified With An Asterisk (*). 
1. 
It is assumed that to influence or attempt to influence 
administrative action requires a "communication" with regard to a 
"proceeding." 
Thus, only "proceeding" numbers which have been the 
subject of communications (including agreeing to hearing dates, 
filing pleadings, etc.) are listed. 
None of the proceedings are 
rate making proceedings in the usual sense of the term. 
However, 
the question of whether other proceedings before the Public 
Utilities Commission are "quasi-legislative" or "quasi-judicial" is 
often a difficult question which has only been partially clarified 
by regulations issued to date and which may be subject to dispute in 
many instances. 
To be conservative, in all questionable cases the 
proceeding has arbitrarily been treated as being quasi-legislative 
and some or all of the proceedings listed may actually be entirely 
judicial in nature. 
For this reason, much or all of the amounts shown 
for payments received and payments made may actually be erroneously 
reported because not in any way connected with attempts to influence 
"administrative action". 
2. 
In Column (a) on page 1, silari has been allocated by 
computing the number of days covered by the report, excluding 
Saturdays and Sundays (but not excluding holidays, vacation days or 
days not worked because of illness) and, assuming that each day is 
a 7-1/2 bour day, dividing the total into the hours expended in 
"connection", directly or indirectly, with what might possibly be 
considered to be lobbying activities to Qbtain a percentage which, 
in turn, was applied to the salary received for the period covered 
by the report. 
This appears to be the only reasonably practical 
method and almost certainly allocates a larger portion of salary to 
"lobbying" than is actually the case. 
3. 
All amounts shown in Column (c) on page 1 are either advances 
or airline tickets (a personal maintenance expense) charged directly 
to the employers account. 
In addition to salary and advance(s) shown 
under Columns (a) and (c), on page 1, other various fringe benefits 
were received, such as medical insurance, etc., the value of which 
is impossible and impractical to calculate or obtain for purposes 
of the lobbyist report and therefore have not been included. 
Also 
since only a portion of my time as an attorney-at-law is spent in 
activities related to the handling of proceedings before the 
California Public Utilities Commission, the amount shown as an advance 
is an allocated portion of total advances based upon ~stimate of .", 
~mount reasonably necessary to cover personal expense~ to be incurred. 
" 
~, 
: 
. 
" 
. 
4. 
To the extent possible, all disbursement items are posted 
and reported according to date incurred rather than date paid. 
Personal maintenance expenses, including reimbursement for use of 
personal automobiles, are reported in amounts reimbursed by employer 
since no other possible or practical method is available and any 
attempt to use any other basis would result in providing less 
accurate information.

sections.question_synthetic: The Political Reform Division seeks guidance on whether lobbyist employers filing consolidated periodic reports should be permitted to do so, specifically addressing whether the Southern California Rock Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association can file consolidated reports and what general guidelines should apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that parent corporations and wholly-owned subsidiaries may file consolidated periodic reports, but all other consolidated reporting requires specific FPPC approval. Joint ventures may be permitted to file as single entities, while the specific associations mentioned should file separate reports pending further determination.

OCR quality issues affect some text readability, particularly in later sections containing Forms 61A and 650. Primary Q&C sections identified from April 5, 1978 response letter. Additional forms and attachments are included but are less relevant to the core advice question.

Explanation With Respect To Items Identified With An Asterisk (*). 
1. 
It is assumed that to influence or attempt to influence 
administrative action requires a "communication" with regard to a 
"proceeding." 
Thus, only "proceeding" numbers which have been the 
subject of communications (including agreeing to hearing dates, 
filing pleadings, etc.) are listed. 
None of the proceedings are 
rate making proceedings in the usual sense of the term. 
However, 
the question of whether other proceedings before the Public 
Utilities Commission are "quasi-legislative" or "quasi-judicial" is 
often a difficult question which has only been partially clarified 
by regulations issued to date and which may be subject to dispute in 
many instances. 
To be conservative, in all questionable cases the 
proceeding has arbitrarily been treated as being quasi-legislative 
and some or all of the proceedings listed may actually be entirely 
judicial in nature. 
For this reason, much or all of the amounts shown 
for payments received and payments made may actually be erroneously 
reported because not in any way connected with attempts to influence 
"administrative action". 
2. 
In Column (a) on page 1, silari has been allocated by 
computing the number of days covered by the report, excluding 
Saturdays and Sundays (but not excluding holidays, vacation days or 
days not worked because of illness) and, assuming that each day is 
a 7-1/2 bour day, dividing the total into the hours expended in 
"connection", directly or indirectly, with what might possibly be 
considered to be lobbying activities to Qbtain a percentage which, 
in turn, was applied to the salary received for the period covered 
by the report. 
This appears to be the only reasonably practical 
method and almost certainly allocates a larger portion of salary to 
"lobbying" than is actually the case. 
3. 
All amounts shown in Column (c) on page 1 are either advances 
or airline tickets (a personal maintenance expense) charged directly 
to the employers account. 
In addition to salary and advance(s) shown 
under Columns (a) and (c), on page 1, other various fringe benefits 
were received, such as medical insurance, etc., the value of which 
is impossible and impractical to calculate or obtain for purposes 
of the lobbyist report and therefore have not been included. 
Also 
since only a portion of my time as an attorney-at-law is spent in 
activities related to the handling of proceedings before the 
California Public Utilities Commission, the amount shown as an advance 
is an allocated portion of total advances based upon ~stimate of .",

embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to a January 27, 1978 request regarding the appropriateness of consolidated periodic reporting by lobbyist employers under Government Code Section 86108(a). The FPPC provides guidelines distinguishing when consolidated reporting is permissible (parent-subsidiary relationships) versus when it requires specific approval (other entities).
